verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And there was a certain disciple at Damascus, named Ananias; and to him said the Lord in a vision, Ananias.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he said, Behold, I am here, Lord.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the Lord said unto him, Arise, and go into the street which is called Straight, and inquire in the house of Judas for one called Saul, of Tarsus: for, behold, he prayeth, and hath seen in a vision a man named Ananias coming in, and putting his hand on him, that he might receive his sight.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What may be the reason that He neither drew any one of high authority and importance, nor caused such to be forthcoming for the purpose of instructing Paul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was, because it was not meet that he should be induced by men, but only by Christ Himself: as in fact this man taught him nothing, but merely baptized him; for, as soon as baptized (φωτισθείς), he was to draw upon himself the grace of the Spirit, by his zeal and exceeding earnestness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that Ananias was no very distinguished person, is plain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, “the Lord,” it says, “spake unto him in a vision, and Ananias answered and said, Lord, I have heard by many of this man, how much evil he hath done to Thy saints at Jerusalem.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if he spoke in objection to Him, much more would he have done so, had He sent an Angel.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this is why, in the former instance, neither is Philip told what the matter is; but he sees the Angel, and then the Spirit bids him go near to the chariot.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe here how the Lord relieves him of his fear: “He is blind,” saith He, “and prayeth, and art thou afraid?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the same way Moses also is afraid: so that the words betokened that he was afraid, and shrunk from the task, not that he did not believe.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He said,” have heard from many concerning this man.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What sayest thou?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God speaketh, and thou hesitatest?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They did not yet well know the power of Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And here he hath authority from the chief priests to bind all that call on Thy name.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How was that known?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is likely that they, being in fear, made minute enquiries.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not say this, as thinking that Christ does not know the fact, but, “such being the case, how,” says he, “can these things be?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As in fact those (in the Gospel) say, “Who can be saved?”—(Mark x.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is done, in order that Paul may believe him that shall come to him: “he hath seen in a vision:” it hath showed him beforehand: “he prayeth,” saith (the Lord): fear not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe, He speaks not to him of the success achieved: teaching us not to speak of our achievements.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, though He saw him afraid, for all this He said it not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Thou shalt not be disbelieved:” “he hath seen,” saith He, “in a vision a man (named) Ananias:” for this is why it was “in a vision,” namely, because he was blind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And not even the exceeding wonderfulness of the thing took possession of the disciple’s mind, so greatly was he afraid.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe: Paul being blind, in this way He restored to sight.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But the Lord said unto him, Go thy way: for he is a chosen vessel unto Me, to bear My name before the Gentiles, and kings, and the children of Israel: for I will show him how great things he must suffer for My name’s sake.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Not only,” saith He, “shall he be a believer, but even a teacher, and great boldness shall he show: ‘before Gentiles and kings’—such shall be the spread of the doctrine!—that just as He astonished (him) by the former, so He may (startle him even more) by the latter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Ananias went, and entered into the house, and laid his hands upon him, and said, Brother Saul”—he straightway addresses him as a friend by that name—“Jesus, Who appeared unto thee in the way in which thou camest”—and yet Christ had not told him this, but he learnt it from the Spirit—“hath sent me unto thee, that thou mayest receive thy sight, and be filled with the Holy Ghost.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As he said this, he laid his hands upon him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And immediately there fell from his eyes as it had been scales.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Some say this was a sign of his blindness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why did he not blind his eyes (entirely)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was more wonderful, that, with his eyes open, he did not see: (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8) which was just his case in respect of the Law, until the Name of Jesus was put on him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he received sight forthwith, and arose, and was baptized.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And having taken food, he recovered strength.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was faint, therefore, both from his journey and from his fear; both from hunger, and from dejection of mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wishing therefore to deepen his dejection, He made the man blind until the coming of Ananias: and, that he might not imagine the blindness to be (only) fancy, this is the reason of the scales.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He needed no other teaching: that which had befallen was made teaching (to him).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he was with the disciples which were at Damascus certain days.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And straightway in the synagogues he preached Jesus, that He is the Son of God.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See, straightway he was a teacher in the synagogues.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was not ashamed of the change, was not afraid while the very things in which he was glorious afore-time, the same he destroyed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Even from his first appearance on the stage here was a man, death-dealing, ready for deeds of blood: seest thou what a manifest sign (was here)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And with this very thing, he put all in fear: for, said they, Hither also is he come for this very thing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But all that heard him were amazed, and said: Is not this he that destroyed them which called on this name in Jerusalem, and came hither for that intent, that he might bring them bound unto the chief priests?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Saul increased the more in strength, and confounded the Jews which dwelt at Damascus, proving that this is very Christ.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As one learned in the Law, he stopped their mouths, and suffered them not to speak.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They thought they were rid of disputation in such matters, in getting rid of Stephen, and they found another, more vehement than Stephen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us look at what relates to Ananias.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Lord said not to him, Converse with him, and catechize him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if, when He said, “He prayeth, and hath seen a man laying his hands upon him,” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He did not persuade him, much less had He said this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that he shall not disbelieve thee, “he hath seen in a vision.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how in the former instance neither is Philip told all immediately.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Fear not, He saith: “for this man is a chosen vessel for Me. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He more than sufficiently released him of his fear, if the case be so that this man shall be so zealous in our cause, as even to suffer many things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And justly he is called “a vessel” (or, instrument)—for reason shows that evil is not a physical quality: “a vessel of election” (or, chosen instrument), He saith; for we choose that which is approved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And let not any imagine, that (Ananias) speaks in unbelief of what was told him, as imagining that Christ was deceived: far from it! but affrighted and trembling, he did not even attend to what was said, at hearing the name of Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover, the Lord does not tell that He has blinded him: at the mention of his name fear had prepossessed his soul: “see,” he says, “to whom Thou art betraying me: ‘and hither for this very purpose is he come, to bind all that call upon Thy Name.’

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I fear, lest he take me to Jerusalem: why dost Thou cast me into the mouth of the lion?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is terrified, even while he speaks these words; that from every quarter we may learn the energetic character (ἀρετήν) of the man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that these things should be spoken by Jews, were nothing wonderful: but that these (the believers) are so terrified, it is a most mighty proof of the power of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both the fear is shown, and the obedience greater after the fear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there was indeed need of strength.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since He says, “a vessel of election,” that thou mayest not imagine that God is to do all, He adds, “to bear My Name before Gentiles and kings, and the children of Israel.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ananias has heard what he most desired—that against the Jews also he will take his stand: this above all gave him courage.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For I,” saith He, “will show him how great things he must suffer for My Name’s sake.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At the same time also this is said by way of putting Ananias to the blush: If he, that was so frantic, shall suffer all things, and thou not willing even to baptize him!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “It is well,” saith he: “let him continue blind” (this is why he says these words): “he is blind: why dost Thou at all bid me open his eyes, that he may bind (men) again?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Fear not the future: for that opening of his eyes he will use not against you, but for you (with reference to that saying, “That he may receive his sight” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12), these words are spoken): for not only will he do you no harm, but he “will suffer many things.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what is wonderful indeed is, that he shall first know “how great things he shall suffer,” and then shall take the field against the perils.—“Brother Saul, the Lord Jesus”—he saith not, “Who made thee blind,” but, “Who appeared with thee in the way, hath sent me unto thee that thou mayest receive thy sight” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17): observe this man also, how he utters nothing boastful, but just as Peter said in the case of the lame man, “Why look ye on us, as though by our own power or holiness we had made him to walk,” (ch. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12) so here also he saith, “Jesus, Who appeared unto thee.” (b) Or, (he saith it) that the other may believe: and he saith not, He that was crucified, the Son of God, He that doeth wonders: but what?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He that appeared unto thee:” (speaking) from what the other knew: as Christ also added no more, neither said, I am Jesus, the Crucified, the Risen: but what?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whom thou persecutest.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ananias said not, “The persecuted,” that he may not seem as it were to rave over him (ἐπενθουσί& 139·ν), to deride him, “Who appeared unto thee in the way:” and yet He did not (visibly) appear, but was seen by the things done.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And immediately he added, wishing to draw a veil over the accusation: “That thou mayest receive thy sight.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I came not to reprove the past, but to bestow the gift: “that thou mayest receive thy sight, and be filled with the Holy Ghost.” (a) With hands laid on, he spake these words.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And immediately there fell from his eyes,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 18): a double blindness is removed.—And why saith it, “Having taken food, he was strengthened?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because they that are in such case become relaxed: he had no heart to partake of food before, until he obtained the mighty gifts. (c) It seems to me, that both Paul and Cornelius, at the very instant when the words were spoken, received the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet (in this case) the giver was no great one.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So true is it, that there was naught of man’s in the things done, nor aught was done by man, but God was present, the Doer of these things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And at the same time (the Lord) both teaches him to think modestly of himself, in that He does not bring him to the Apostles who were so admired, and shows that there is nothing of man here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was not filled, however, with the Spirit which works signs: that in this way also his faith might be shown; for he wrought no miracles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And straightway,” it says, “in the synagogues he preached Jesus”—(v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20) not that He is risen—not this: no, nor that He liveth: but what? immediately he strictly expounded the doctrine—“that this is the Son of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And all that heard him were amazed,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They were reduced to utter incredulity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet they ought not to have wondered only, but to worship and reverence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Is not this he,” etc. He had not merely been a persecutor, but “destroyed them which called on this Name”—they did not say, “on Jesus;” for hatred, they could not bear even to hear His name—and what is more marvellous still, “and came hither for this purpose,” etc. “We cannot say, that he associated with the Apostles before.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See by how many (witnesses) he is confessed to have been of the number of the enemies!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Paul not only was not confounded by these things, nor hid his face for shame, but “increased the more in strength, and confounded the Jews” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22), i.e. put them to silence, left them nothing to say for themselves, “proving, that this is very Christ.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Teaching,” it says: for this man was a teacher.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And after that many days were fulfilled, the Jews took counsel to kill him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Jews again resort to that valid argument (ἰσχυρὸν συλλογισμόν) of theirs, not now seeking false-accusers and false-witnesses; they cannot wait for these now: but what do they?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They set about it by themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as they see the affair on the increase, they do not even use the form of a trial.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But their laying await was known of Saul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they watched the gates day and night to kill him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this was more intolerable to them than the miracles which had taken place—than the five thousand, the three thousand, than everything, in short.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe him, how he is delivered, not by (miraculous) grace, but by man’s wisdom—not as the apostles were—(ἐκεἴνοι, ch. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 19) that thou mayest learn the energetic (ἀρετὴν) character of the man, how he shines even without miracles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then the disciples took him by night,” that the affair might not be suspected, “and let him down by the wall in a basket.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then? having escaped such a danger, does he flee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By no means, but goes where he kindled them to greater rage.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation, v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And straightway in the synagogues he preached Jesus”—for he was accurate in the faith—“that this is the Son of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But all that heard him were amazed,” etc., for indeed it was incredible.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Saul increased,” etc. Therefore “after many days” this happens: viz. the Jews “took counsel to kill him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And their laying await was known of Saul.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What does this mean?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is likely that for awhile he did not choose to depart thence, though many, perhaps, besought him; but when he learnt it, then he permitted his disciples: for he had disciples immediately.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then the disciples,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of this occurrence he says: “The ethnarch of Aretas the king kept the city of the Damascenes with a garrison, desiring to apprehend me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe the Writer here, that he does not tell the story ambitiously, and so as to show what an important person Paul was, saying, “For they stirred up the king,” and so forth: but only, “Then the disciples took him by night, and let him down by the wall—in a basket:” for they sent him out alone, and none with him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it was well they did this: the consequence being, that he showed himself to the Apostles in Jerusalem.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now they sent him out, as bound to provide for his safety by flight: but he did just the contrary—he leaped into the midst of those who were mad against him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This it is to be on fire, this to be fervent indeed!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From that day forth he knew all the commands which the Apostles had heard: “Except a man take up his cross, and follow Me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The very fact that he had been slower to come than the rest made him more zealous: for “to whom much is forgiven” (Luke vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 47) the same will love more, so that the later he came, the more he loved: * * * and having done ten thousand wrongs, he thought he could never do enough to cast the former deeds into the shade.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Proving” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22), it says: i.e. with mildness teaching.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe, they did not say to him, Thou art he that destroyed: why art thou changed? for they were ashamed: but they said it to themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he would have said to them, This very thing ought to teach you, as in fact he does thus plead in his speech before Agrippa.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us imitate this, man: let us bear our souls in our hands ready to confront all dangers.—(That he fled from Damascus) this was no cowardice: he preserved himself for the preaching.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Had he been a coward, he would not have gone to Jerusalem, would not immediately have commenced teaching: he would have abated somewhat of his vehemence: for he had been taught by the fate of Stephen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was no coward, but he was also prudent (οἰκονομικός) (in husbanding himself).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore he thought it no great thing to die for the Gospel’s sake, unless he should do this to great advantage: willing not even to see Christ, Whom most of all he longed to see, while the work of his stewardship among men was not yet complete.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such ought to be the soul of a Christian.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From his first appearance from the very outset, the character of Paul declared itself: nay even before this, even in the things which he did “not according to knowledge” (Rom. x.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 2), it was not by man’s reasoning that he was moved to act as he did.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if, so long afterwards, he was content not to depart, much more at the beginning of his trading voyage, when he had but just left the harbor!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many things Christ leaves to be done by (ordinary) human wisdom, that we may learn that (his disciples) were men, that it was not all everywhere to be done by grace: for otherwise they would have been mere motionless logs: but in many things they managed matters themselves.—This is not less than martyrdom,—to shrink from no suffering for the sake of the salvation of the many.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing so delights God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again will I repeat what I have often said: and I repeat it, because I do exceedingly desire it: as Christ also did the same, when discoursing concerning forgiveness: “When ye pray, forgive if ye have aught against any man:” (Mark xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 25.) and again to Peter He said, “I say not unto thee, Forgive until seven times, but until seventy-times seven.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Himself in fact forgives the transgressions against Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So do we also, because we know that this is the very goal of Christianity, continually discourse thereof.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing is more frigid than a Christian, who cares not for the salvation of others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou canst not here plead poverty: for she that cast down the two mites, shall be thine accuser.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Peter said, “Silver and gold have I none.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Paul was so poor, that he was often hungered, and wanted necessary food.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou canst not plead lowness of birth: for they too were ignoble men, and of ignoble parents.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou canst not allege want of education: for they too were “unlearned men.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Even if thou be a slave therefore and a runaway slave, thou canst perform thy part: for such was Onesimus: yet see to what Paul calls him, and to how great honor he advances him: “that he may communicate with me,” he says, “in my bonds.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou canst not plead infirmity: for such was Timothy, having often infirmities; for, says the apostle, “Use a little wine for thy stomach’s sake, and thine often infirmities.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Every one can profit his neighbor, if he will fulfil his part.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See ye not the unfruitful trees, how strong they are, how fair, how large also, and smooth, and of great height?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if we had a garden; we should much rather have pomegranates, or fruitful olive trees: for the others are for delight to the eye, not for profit, which in them is but small.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such are those men who only consider their own interest: nay, not such even since these persons are fit only for burning: whereas those trees are useful both for building and for the safety of those within.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such too were those Virgins, chaste indeed, and decent, and modest, but profitable to none (Matt. xxv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1) wherefore they are burned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such are they who have not nourished Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For observe that none of those are charged with particular sins of their own, with fornication, for instance, or with perjury; in short, with no sin but the having been of no use to another.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such was he who buried his talent, showing indeed a blameless life, but not being useful to another. (ib.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How can such an one be a Christian?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, if the leaven being mixed up with the flour did not change the whole into its own nature, would such a thing be leaven?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, if a perfume shed no sweet odor on those who approach it, could we call it a perfume?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say not, “It is impossible for me to induce others (to become Christians)”—for if thou art a Christian, it is impossible but that it should be so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as the natural properties of things cannot be gainsaid, so it is here: the thing is part of the very nature of the Christian.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not insult God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To say, that the sun cannot shine, would be to insult Him: to say that a Christian cannot do good, is to insult God, and call Him a liar.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is easier for the sun not to give heat, nor to shine, than for the Christian not to send forth light: it is easier for the light to be darkness, than for this to be so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tell me not that it is impossible: the contrary is the impossible.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not insult God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we once get our own affairs in a right state, the other will certainly follow as a natural and necessary consequence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not possible for the light of a Christian to be hid; not possible for a lamp so conspicuous as that to be concealed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not be careless.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, as the profit from virtue reaches both to ourselves, and to those who are benefited by it: so from vice there is a two-fold loss, reaching both to ourselves, and to those who are injured by it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let there be (if you will) some private man, who has suffered numberless ills from some one, and let no one take his part, yet let that man still return good offices; what teaching so mighty as this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What words, or what exhortations could equal it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What wrath were it not enough to extinguish and soften?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Knowing therefore these things, let us hold fast to virtue, as knowing that it is not possible to be saved otherwise, than by passing through this present life in doing these good works, that we may also obtain the good things which are to come, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father together with the Holy Spirit be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
